title: When You're the Only One Who Fights Fair
dek: You’ve been told your anger is the problem. You’ve learned to argue with structured sentences, breath pauses, while they lob grenades. This isn’t a guide to being calmer. It’s a reckoning with what your fairness is costing you and why the body always knows exactly who started the fight—and who never intended to play fair in the first place.
bodyMarkdown:
Why are you still waiting for an apology that will never come?

### The Script You Were Handed

You were taught to be the reasonable one before you could tie your own shoes. A parent’s glare, a teacher’s sharp sigh, the thousand micro-corrections that bent your spine into a question mark: *Why can’t you just get along?* So you learned the rules of engagement. Listen without interrupting. Use “I” statements. Breathe before you speak. You read the books, you did the therapy, you swallowed the hot bile of rebuttal and replaced it with a measured tone. And now you sit across from someone who has never read a single chapter, who flings accusations like wet clay, and you still try to sculpt arguments into neat paragraphs while they’re smashing your walls with their bare hands.

The body remembers this training. Feel it now: the way your jaw sets when you’re mid-sentence and they cut you off. A clench that starts in the molars and radiates down the sides of your neck, a tightness in the trapezius like you’re bracing against a blow that already landed twenty years ago. You’ve been told your anger is the real problem—that if you could just metabolize it better, the fights would stop. But look at the evidence. You’ve been the designated diplomat in a war you didn’t start, and every time you agree to the rules, you cede another inch of your own territory.

### When Fairness Is a Cage

Here’s the counterintuitive snarl: fighting fair is not a virtue when you’re the only one holding the rulebook. Fairness requires two participants who share a basic commitment to not destroying each other. Without that shared floor, your “fairness” isn’t morality—it’s a leash. You hand your opponent the weapon of your own restraint. They know you won’t mention the real wound, the ugly thing they did last Tuesday, because that would be “below the belt.” So they jab at your soft parts while you stand there, footwork perfect, guard up, waiting for a referee who never enters the ring.

The physical cost of this cage is specific and cumulative. After a “discussion” where you stayed calm and they didn’t, notice where your body stores the residue. Low back pulsing with a dull ache, as if you’ve been carrying a child on one hip for hours. A sour constriction in the throat, the same place that tightens when you force down tears. Your hands might be ice-cold while a fire blazes behind your sternum—a contradiction that exhaustion explains better than your therapist ever did. This isn’t a failure of emotional regulation; it’s the body keeping score while your mind runs elaborate justifications.

Thich Nhat Hanh, the Vietnamese Zen teacher, talked about watering seeds. Every interaction waters a seed in your consciousness—the seed of anger, the seed of understanding, the seed of love. He said that if you water the seed of anger continually, it grows strong and pushes out everything else. But he also said something more subtle: you can water the seed of your own suppression and call it peace. When you “fight fair” in a rigged game, you’re drenching the seed of self-betrayal. You’re cultivating a garden of poison ivy and telling yourself it’s a zen rock garden. The other person’s seed of aggression is being watered by your compliance, and no one notices because your voice stays soft.

### The Body Remembers Every Flinch

Before the words even start, your body knows who you’re dealing with. You walk into the room and your shoulders lift a half-inch toward your ears—a prehistoric gesture of shielding the jugular. Your diaphragm seizes, making your breath shallow and fast. You might feel a flutter in the belly, not butterflies but the frantic wings of a moth trapped in a jar. This is the somatic alarm system you’ve been trained to ignore in the name of being fair.

There’s a moment, usually just after they’ve said something cruel but deniable, when your body sends a clear signal: heat flaring up the back of your neck like a sunburn from inside, a sudden buzz in your palms, a clench in the pelvic floor so deep it might as well be speaking in tongues. You’ve learned to override it with a head-nod, a reflective “I hear what you’re saying,” while your throat constricts around the words you won’t allow. Harriet Lerner, in her decades of work on anger and intimacy, calls anger a signal—a red light on the dashboard saying something under the hood is wrong. But when you’re the only one fighting fair, the signal isn’t just about this argument. It’s an alarm that’s been ringing since you first learned that being good meant swallowing your own defense.

Lerner’s central idea is that anger isn’t a pathology; it’s a tool that tells you exactly where your boundaries are being pierced. The problem, she says, isn’t your anger—it’s what you’ve been trained to do with it. In the unfair fight, you use your anger to fuel more careful phrasing, more diplomatic pivoting. Meanwhile, the other person uses their anger as a sledgehammer and you’re still offering them a hand mirror to examine their reflection. Lerner would call this a dance where you’re always leading and they’re always stepping on your toes, and you apologize for their clumsiness.

### The Grenade Thrower’s Game

Let’s get concrete. You’re in the kitchen. They bring up an old grievance, one you thought was resolved, but they twist the details until you barely recognize the event. You say, “I feel hurt when you frame it that way.” They fire back, “So now I’m the villain? You’re always the victim.” Your pulse spikes. You try to clarify—not to win, but to be understood. “I’m not saying you’re a villain. I’m saying this specific thing you did affected me.” They pivot: “Why do you have to pathologize everything? I was just upset.” And then the grenade, the one that always empties the room: “You’re so sensitive. You ruin every evening with this.”

You stand there, heart hammering, mind racing for a fair response to an argument that has no fair framework. Your body screams: tight chest, a lump in the throat like a swallowed stone, a prickling behind the eyes that you refuse to let become tears. You’ve been here before. The unfair fighter doesn’t want a resolution; they want an audience for their own emotional dumping. Your fairness is the stage, the spotlight, and the exit sign all at once.

Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on emotional abuse and invalidation, pinpoints a wound often overlooked: the damage isn’t always the original betrayal, but the repeated refusal to acknowledge it afterward. She writes that for many, the deepest cut is the invalidation that follows—the other person’s insistence that your pain is manufactured, that your memory is faulty, that you’re just “trying to start something.” When you fight fair, you leave yourself wide open to this secondary wound. You hand them a blueprint of your interior, point to the tender spots, and then act surprised when they jab a thumb in and twist.

### A Practice: Somatic Stopwatch

You can do this in under five minutes, ideally right after an interaction, or even imagining one that still hooks you. Sit somewhere you won’t be disturbed. Plant both feet on the ground and let your hands rest on your thighs, palms up. Close your eyes. Now, recall the last unfair fight. Not the words—skip the script. Go straight to the first physical signal that something was off. Was it the jaw? The gut? A sudden heat in your ears? Name it silently: *There’s the clench. There’s the hollow drop in my chest.*

Now, imagine you’re holding a stopwatch that measures not seconds, but the distance between your gut reaction and your verbal response. In that gap, there’s a hairline fracture in your reality. In that split moment, you chose to override your body’s warning. Feel that moment now—the bracing, the breath held. Exhale it out. Then ask yourself, with as little drama as possible: “What would have happened if I had said, right then, ‘That doesn’t land right’ and stopped talking?” Don’t fix the conversation in your mind; just let your body feel the release of not managing their emotions. Try it three breaths. The body knows this relief, even in fantasy. That’s the seed worth watering.

If your system is so revved that even this short practice feels like touching a live wire, you might consider recruiting a little botanical support. There’s no medal for raw-dogging chronic hypervigilance. Something like [Gaia Herbs Ashwagandha Root (60 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0719D8GCB?tag=spankyspinola-20) can take the razor edge off your cortisol cascade without numbing you to the signals. It’s not a substitute for the work, but sometimes a slightly quieter nervous system is the only way you can hear what your anger is actually saying.

### The Apology That Will Never Anchor You

You’ve likely spent years believing that if you could just get the other person to acknowledge what they did—if you could make them see the asymmetry—then something would unlock. You’d feel lighter. The rage would drain. But Engel’s research on what she calls the “impossible apology” says something harder: some people are structurally incapable of offering what you need. Not because they’re deeply evil, but because their own shame armor prevents them from ever saying the simple words that would stitch you back together. When you keep fighting fair, a part of you is still angling for that apology, still trying to model the behavior you wish they’d mirror. The tragedy is they see your modeling not as a gift, but as an admission ticket to keep playing a game they’ve rigged.

This is where the body’s memory becomes a liability you can transform. The tension in your shoulders? That’s not just the current fight; it’s the sediment of every time you were reasonable and received only contempt. The ache in your thighs from bracing under the table? That’s your body doing a job your mind won’t admit needs to be done—protecting you from a threat that’s real, even if it arrives wrapped in a loved one’s voice. Thomas Moore, in a book that has nothing to do with self-help and everything to do with the underlayers of the psyche, suggests that so-called disturbances of the soul are often the exact right response to an unwell environment. [Care of the Soul — Thomas Moore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0060921269?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a slower, more mythological lens: maybe your anger isn’t a glitch but a necessary haunting that, if listened to, could lead you out of the haunted house.

### FAQ: You Ask, The Body Answers

**“But if I stop fighting fair, won’t I become just like them?”**  
Fairness isn’t a personality trait; it’s a practice done with a partner. Stopping fairness doesn’t mean becoming cruel. It means stopping the pretense that you’re in a mutual process. You can say, “I’m not going to continue this conversation when you speak to me that way,” and leave. That’s not unfair—it’s a boundary. The fear of becoming “just like them” is a legacy of being the family peacemaker; it’s a ghost that deserves a proper burial, not an ongoing haunting.

**“What if I’m wrong? What if I’m actually the unfair one?”**  
Notice this question lives in your body as a sickness in the stomach, a rapid scanning for flaws in your own argument. When you’re the only one fighting fair, you’re also the only one who does this excruciating self-audit. The other person rarely pauses for a moment of genuine self-reflection. Ask your body: right now, am I braced for punishment? That flinch is old, likely older than this relationship. The very fact that you’re torturing yourself with the question is evidence that you’re carrying a fairness standard that is not evenly applied.

**“How do I sleep after these fights? I’m just up all night cycling.”**  
When the argument loops at 3 a.m., your body is stuck in a feedback loop of adrenaline and rumination. The mind replays the script, hoping for a different ending; the body stays rigid, as if sleep is a battle you’ll lose. A blunt intervention: before bed, write down one sentence your body tried to say during the fight, something like “My jaw hurts because I’m holding back a scream.” Then, if the night-waking has become routine, consider a formula that addresses the physiological kick without dragging you under a chemical blanket. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) is designed for exactly this—a blend that targets the stress-insomnia spiral without the morning hangover. But the deeper work is to stop asking your mind to solve a problem your body is already answering: this person is not safe for you, and your system knows it before your decency will admit it.

### The Fight You Walk Away From

There is a particular clarity that arrives not when you finally win an argument, but when you stop chasing a resolution that was never on the table. I’ve written elsewhere about [what your anger is actually protecting](https://theoraclelover.com), and it might surprise you: often, your anger isn’t hiding the tender wound of betrayal. It’s hiding the sheer exhaustion of having to translate your own humanity to someone who has checked the “out of order” box on their receipt of you. The unfair fight is a language game, and you’ve been trying to translate poetry into a language that only has words for commands.

Your body, that tireless record-keeper, will eventually stop sending you polite memos. It will scream. It might develop migraines that feel like a railway spike, a gut that refuses to digest
